title: The 20th Century
overview:
The early 20th century saw Britain at the height of its power as a global superpower, but this era was defined by two devastating world wars. The First World War (1914-18) involved millions of soldiers from across the British Empire. The partition of Ireland followed in 1922. A period of recovery and the Great Depression preceded the Second World War (1939-45), in which Britain stood almost alone against Nazi Germany before the Allies achieved final victory.

This lesson covers the First World War, the partition of Ireland, the inter-war period, and the Second World War.

study_sections.heading: The First World War (1914-1918)
study_sections.content:
The early 20th century was a time of optimism in Britain. The nation, with its expansive Empire, well-admired navy, thriving industry and strong political institutions, was a global superpower. Financial help for the unemployed, old-age pensions and free school meals were introduced.

This era of optimism was cut short when war broke out between European nations. On 28 June 1914, Archduke Franz Ferdinand of Austria was assassinated. This set off a chain of events leading to the First World War (1914-18). Other factors such as growing nationalism, increasing militarism, imperialism, and the division of Europe into two camps all set the conditions for war.

Britain was part of the Allied Powers, which included France, Russia, Japan, Belgium, Serbia, and later Greece, Italy, Romania and the United States. The whole of the British Empire was involved - more than a million Indians fought on behalf of Britain and around 40,000 were killed. Men from the West Indies, Africa, Australia, New Zealand and Canada also fought with the British. The Allies fought against the Central Powers - mainly Germany, the Austro-Hungarian Empire, the Ottoman Empire and later Bulgaria.

Millions of people were killed or wounded, with more than 2 million British casualties. One battle, the British attack on the Somme in July 1916, resulted in about 60,000 British casualties on the first day alone.

The First World War ended at 11.00 am on 11th November 1918 with victory for Britain and its allies.

study_sections.heading: The Partition of Ireland
study_sections.content:
In 1913, the British government promised 'Home Rule' for Ireland - a self-governing Ireland with its own parliament but still part of the UK. The proposal was opposed by Protestants in the north of Ireland, who threatened to resist by force.

The outbreak of the First World War led the British government to postpone any changes in Ireland. Irish Nationalists were not willing to wait and in 1916 there was an uprising (the Easter Rising) against the British in Dublin. The leaders of the uprising were executed under military law. A guerrilla war against the British army and the police in Ireland followed.

In 1921 a peace treaty was signed and in 1922 Ireland became two countries. The six counties in the north, which were mainly Protestant, remained part of the UK under the name Northern Ireland. The rest of Ireland became the Irish Free State with its own government. It became a republic in 1949.

There were people in both parts of Ireland who disagreed with the split. They still wanted Ireland to be one independent country. Years of disagreement led to a terror campaign in Northern Ireland and elsewhere. The conflict between those wishing for full Irish independence and those wishing to remain loyal to the British government is often referred to as 'the Troubles'.

study_sections.heading: The Inter-War Period and Great Depression
study_sections.content:
In the 1920s, many people's living conditions got better. There were improvements in public housing and new homes were built in many towns and cities.

However, in 1929, the world entered the 'Great Depression' and some parts of the UK suffered mass unemployment. The effects of the depression of the 1930s were felt differently in different parts of the UK. The traditional heavy industries such as shipbuilding were badly affected but new industries - including the automobile and aviation industries - developed. As prices generally fell, those in work had more money to spend. Car ownership doubled from 1 million to 2 million between 1930 and 1939.

The BBC started radio broadcasts in 1922 and began the world's first regular television service in 1936.

study_sections.heading: The Second World War - The Start
study_sections.content:
Adolf Hitler came to power in Germany in 1933. He believed that the conditions imposed on Germany by the Allies after the First World War were unfair; he also wanted to conquer more land for the German people. He set about renegotiating treaties, building up arms, and testing Germany's military strength in nearby countries.

The British government tried to avoid another war. However, when Hitler invaded Poland in 1939, Britain and France declared war in order to stop his aggression.

The war was initially fought between the Axis powers (fascist Germany and Italy and the Empire of Japan) and the Allies. The main countries on the allied side were the UK, France, Poland, Australia, New Zealand, Canada, and the Union of South Africa.

Having occupied Austria and invaded Czechoslovakia, Hitler took control of Belgium and the Netherlands. Then, in 1940, German forces defeated allied troops and advanced through France. At this time of national crisis, Winston Churchill became Prime Minister and Britain's war leader.

study_sections.heading: Dunkirk and the Battle of Britain
study_sections.content:
As France fell, the British decided to evacuate British and French soldiers from France. Many civilian volunteers in small pleasure and fishing boats helped the Navy to rescue more than 300,000 men from the beaches around Dunkirk. Although many lives and a lot of equipment were lost, the evacuation was a success. The evacuation gave rise to the phrase 'the Dunkirk spirit'.

From the end of June 1940 until the German invasion of the Soviet Union in June 1941, Britain and the Empire stood almost alone against Nazi Germany.

Hitler wanted to invade Britain, but first needed to control the air. The Germans waged an air campaign against Britain, but the British resisted with their fighter planes and eventually won the crucial aerial battle against the Germans, called 'the Battle of Britain', in the summer of 1940. The most important planes used by the Royal Air Force were the Spitfire and the Hurricane, which were designed and built in Britain.

Despite this victory, the German air force continued bombing London and other British cities at night-time. This was called the Blitz. Coventry was almost totally destroyed and a great deal of damage was done in other cities, especially in the East End of London. Despite the destruction, there was a strong national spirit of resistance. The phrase 'the Blitz spirit' is still used today to describe Britons pulling together in the face of adversity.

Winston Churchill (1874-1965) was Prime Minister from May 1940. He refused to surrender to the Nazis and was an inspirational leader. He made many famous speeches, including: 'We shall fight on the beaches... we shall never surrender' and 'Never in the field of human conflict was so much owed by so many to so few.' He was voted the greatest Briton of all time in 2002.

study_sections.heading: The Second World War - Allied Victory
study_sections.content:
The allied forces gradually gained the upper hand, winning significant victories in North Africa and Italy. The United States entered the war when the Japanese bombed its naval base at Pearl Harbour in December 1941.

On 6 June 1944, allied forces landed in Normandy (this event is often referred to as 'D-Day'). Following victory on the beaches of Normandy, the allied forces pressed on through France and eventually into Germany. The Allies comprehensively defeated Germany in May 1945.

The war against Japan ended in August 1945 when the United States dropped atom bombs on the Japanese cities of Hiroshima and Nagasaki. Scientists led by Ernest Rutherford, working at Manchester and then Cambridge University, were the first to 'split the atom' and took part in the Manhattan Project, which developed the atomic bomb.

Alexander Fleming (1881-1955), born in Scotland, discovered penicillin in 1928 while researching influenza. It was further developed by Howard Florey and Ernst Chain into a usable drug, and by the 1940s was in mass production. Fleming won the Nobel Prize in Medicine in 1945. Penicillin is still used to treat bacterial infections today.

key_facts: Archduke Franz Ferdinand was assassinated on 28 June 1914, triggering the First World War.
The First World War lasted from 1914 to 1918.
Britain was part of the Allied Powers.
More than a million Indians fought on behalf of Britain in WWI.
Around 40,000 Indians were killed in WWI.
There were more than 2 million British casualties in WWI.
The Battle of the Somme in July 1916 resulted in about 60,000 British casualties on the first day.
The First World War ended at 11.00 am on 11th November 1918.
The Easter Rising against the British took place in Dublin in 1916.
A peace treaty was signed in 1921 and Ireland became two countries in 1922.
Northern Ireland (six mainly Protestant counties) remained part of the UK.
The rest of Ireland became the Irish Free State.
The Irish Free State became a republic in 1949.
The conflict in Northern Ireland is often referred to as 'the Troubles'.
The Great Depression began in 1929.
Car ownership doubled from 1 million to 2 million between 1930 and 1939.
The BBC started radio broadcasts in 1922.
The BBC began the world's first regular television service in 1936.
Adolf Hitler came to power in Germany in 1933.
Britain and France declared war on Germany in 1939 after Hitler invaded Poland.
Winston Churchill became Prime Minister in May 1940.
More than 300,000 men were rescued from Dunkirk.
The Battle of Britain was won in the summer of 1940.
The key RAF planes in the Battle of Britain were the Spitfire and the Hurricane.
Coventry was almost totally destroyed during the Blitz.
The United States entered the war after Japan bombed Pearl Harbour in December 1941.
D-Day was on 6 June 1944 when allied forces landed in Normandy.
Germany was defeated in May 1945.
Japan was defeated in August 1945 after atom bombs were dropped on Hiroshima and Nagasaki.
Alexander Fleming discovered penicillin in 1928.
Fleming won the Nobel Prize in Medicine in 1945.
Winston Churchill was voted the greatest Briton of all time in 2002.
memory_hook: Remember WWI with '1914-18, Somme, 11/11': war started 1914, Battle of Somme 1916 (60,000 casualties day one), ended 11am 11 November 1918. For WWII: '1939-45, Dunkirk, D-Day': Hitler invaded Poland 1939, 300,000 rescued at Dunkirk, Battle of Britain 1940, D-Day 6 June 1944, Germany defeated May 1945.
